title: practical english usage - Part 327
content:
For hou>  to ..., ►     111.       For how ever, ►    627.   For learn how to ..* 508.

(sen The uwy how you organise. ..) or The way you organise the work is for you to decide,  How you organise...

(     ... the way how those cats wash ...) or Look at the way those cats wash each other,  Look at how those cats ...

Note that the way and how are not used together.

The way (►  620) can often be used instead of non-interrogative how.

(     .. .-thehow-nwnyvlh?) ‘ It's our wedding anniversary.’ ‘ Congratulations. Which one?

(first, second, etc).

Note that English does not have a special expression to ask for ordinal numbers vocabulary  •  496  in and into, on and onto: prepositions Go and jump  in the river.                         -> - The children keep jumping into the flowerbeds.

the movement - the place where somebody or something will be. Compare: we  think of the movement itself, and in/on when we  think more of the end of or on and onto, to talk about directional movement. We   prefer into/onto when After some verbs (e.g. throw, jump,  push, put, fall) we  can use both in and into, 2 in   and   on   for   movement possible in British English.

Note that into and onto are normally written as single words. On  to is also How does it get onto the roof?

- The cat's on the roof again.

Then she walked into the house.

- She was walking in the garden.

The ball rolled slowly into the goal, (     ... rolled slowly in the goal.) - A moment later the ball was in the goal.

are; and into and onto to talk about directions and destinations - where things We   generally use in and on to talk about the positions of things - where they 1  position      and   direction 496 in   and   into,     on   and   onto:     prepositions 469 ► in   and   for:   time ►    441 in   and   during 386 ► in,  at   and   on:  time 384 ► in,  at   and   on:  place I'll let you know the moment I have some definite information.

the future.

As with other time conjunctions, we  use present tenses when talking about I loved you the instant (that) I saw you.

Telephone me the moment (that) you get the results.

be used in the same way (in both British and American English).

The moment (that), the instant (that), the second (that) and the minute (that) can Directly I walked in the door, I smelt smoke.

I knew something was wrong immediately I arrived.

Tell me immediately you have any news.

to mean ’ as soon as’ .

In British English, immediately and directly can be used as conjunctions, 495 imme     diately,       the    m  oment,       etc:  conjunctions Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  497  indifferent ►    ɔss in   order      that     and   so   that ►    549 in   front     of  and   opposite ►    397 in   front     of   and   before The team's performance has been indifferent all year.

Another meaning is 'not very good’ .

You can't be indifferent to climate change.

He's quite indifferent to people's opinion of his work.

interest in something that other people might be concerned about.

Indifferent is not the opposite of different. It is used to say that one has no 497 indifferent 385 ► in/at    and   to ►    ʒoe in   and   later For the difference between in and to, ►    385.

What have you got on?  Put your coat on.

I stayed in last night.   Come in! (     Come-into!) In and on are used as adverbs for both position and movement.

4 in   and   on   as  adverbs not I broke it in half (     ... into half) And note the expression in half.

Cut the onion in(to) small pieces.

Cut can be followed by into or in.

Can you translate this into Chinese? (     .. .-translate this in Chinese?) not (     .. .-ehanged-in-a-handsome prince.) When she kissed the frog, it changed into a handsome prince.

We   normally use into after verbs suggesting change.

3 into     for   change For arrive at. . ., * 384.

We arrive in Athens at midday, (                 We arrive into Athens...) not                                               or (     He-sat-dewn-into...  /  sat down onto...) He sat down in the armchair, and I sat down on the floor.
